questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Recording acts - inquiry notice from possession
questions.stem: Owner conveyed a farm to Buyer 1, who paid value and immediately moved onto the farm but did not record the deed. Owner then purported to convey the same farm to Buyer 2 for value. Before closing, Buyer 2 drove by the farm and saw Buyer 1 living there, operating equipment, and receiving mail at the farmhouse, but Buyer 2 made no inquiry. The jurisdiction has a notice recording statute protecting subsequent purchasers for value without notice.
questions.call: Who has superior title?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Buyer 2, because Buyer 2 paid value and had no record notice of Buyer 1's deed.
choices.rationale: Record notice is not the only form of notice. Visible possession can create inquiry notice.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Buyer 2, because a notice statute always protects the later deed regardless of possession.
choices.rationale: A notice statute protects only a subsequent purchaser without notice, including inquiry notice.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Buyer 1, but only because Buyer 1 eventually could acquire title by adverse possession.
choices.rationale: Buyer 1 already has superior title through the prior deed; the issue is Buyer 2's notice, not adverse possession.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Buyer 1, because Buyer 2 had inquiry notice from Buyer 1's open possession before taking the deed.
choices.rationale: Possession inconsistent with the record owner can put a purchaser on inquiry notice. A purchaser who fails to inquire is not protected as without notice.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether open possession gives a later purchaser inquiry notice of an unrecorded deed.
explanation.governingRule: Under notice and race-notice systems, a subsequent purchaser must lack actual, record, and inquiry notice. Open and obvious possession by someone other than the grantor can require inquiry into that person's rights.
explanation.application: Buyer 1 was visibly occupying and using the farm before Buyer 2 closed. Buyer 2's failure to inquire means Buyer 2 had inquiry notice and is not protected.
explanation.commonTrap: Checking only the recording office and ignoring facts visible on the land.
explanation.memoryAid: Possession can be notice: ask who is on the land and why.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Rule Against Perpetuities - class gift to children reaching age 25
questions.stem: A grantor conveyed land 'to Anna for life, then to Anna's children who reach age 25.' At the time of the conveyance, Anna was alive and had one child, age 4. The jurisdiction applies the common-law Rule Against Perpetuities and has no saving statute or wait-and-see doctrine.
questions.call: What is the status of the remainder to Anna's children?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is void under the Rule Against Perpetuities because an afterborn child could reach 25 more than 21 years after Anna's death.
choices.rationale: Anna could have another child, and that child might be under age 4 at Anna's death and not reach 25 within 21 years after any life in being.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is valid because all of Anna's children must be identified no later than Anna's death.
choices.rationale: Identification of class members is not enough; each interest must vest, if at all, within the perpetuities period. Reaching 25 can occur too late.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is valid as to the existing child but void only as to afterborn children.
choices.rationale: Under the common-law all-or-nothing rule for class gifts, a gift to a class is void if it might vest too remotely as to any member.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is valid because 25 years is close enough to the 21-year period.
choices.rationale: The common-law rule is strict. Vesting beyond 21 years after lives in being is too remote.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a class gift to a life tenant's children who reach 25 violates the common-law Rule Against Perpetuities.
explanation.governingRule: Under the common-law Rule Against Perpetuities, an interest is void unless it must vest or fail no later than 21 years after a life in being at creation. Class gifts are traditionally treated under an all-or-nothing approach.
explanation.application: Anna might have a child after the conveyance who is a minor when Anna dies and reaches 25 more than 21 years later. Because that possibility exists at creation, the class gift is void.
explanation.commonTrap: Thinking the class closes at Anna's death solves the separate problem of age-contingent vesting.
explanation.memoryAid: Age conditions above 21 often create RAP danger for open classes.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Easement by necessity - termination when necessity ends
questions.stem: A landowner divided a tract, conveying the back parcel to a buyer and retaining the front parcel. At severance, the back parcel had no access to a public road except across the front parcel, so the buyer obtained an easement by necessity over the retained land. Ten years later, the county built a public road directly along the back parcel's boundary, giving the back parcel practical legal access without crossing the front parcel. The buyer continued to use the old route across the front parcel.
questions.call: May the front parcel owner terminate the easement by necessity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because an easement by necessity lasts forever once it arises.
choices.rationale: An easement by necessity lasts only as long as the necessity exists.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because an easement by necessity ends when the necessity ceases.
choices.rationale: The new public road provides access to the back parcel, eliminating the necessity that justified the implied easement.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the buyer used the route for more than ten years.
choices.rationale: Continued use might support prescription only if the elements are met, including adverse use. The stated easement by necessity itself ends with necessity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the original deed expressly reserved a termination right.
choices.rationale: The termination follows from the nature of an easement by necessity and does not require an express reservation.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an easement by necessity continues after alternative legal access becomes available.
explanation.governingRule: An easement by necessity is implied to prevent landlocking when common ownership is severed. It continues only so long as the necessity exists.
explanation.application: The county road gives the back parcel direct legal access, so the necessity for crossing the front parcel has ended.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming all implied easements are permanent regardless of changed access.
explanation.memoryAid: Necessity creates the easement; ending necessity ends it.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Assignment versus sublease - transfer of entire remaining term
questions.stem: A tenant held a five-year commercial lease. With two years remaining, the tenant transferred 'all of tenant's right to possess the premises for the rest of the lease term' to a business owner. The document called the transfer a 'sublease,' but the tenant retained no right to reenter at the end of any shorter period. The original lease did not prohibit transfer. The business owner took possession and then failed to pay rent.
questions.call: How should the transfer be characterized?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As a sublease, because the document used the word 'sublease.'
choices.rationale: Labels do not control. The legal effect depends on the interest transferred and any retained reversion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: As a license, because commercial tenants cannot transfer possessory interests without landlord consent.
choices.rationale: The facts state no transfer prohibition, and a tenant may generally transfer the leasehold interest subject to lease terms.

choices.label: C
choices.text: As an assignment, because the tenant transferred the entire remaining lease term and retained no reversion.
choices.rationale: A transfer of the tenant's entire remaining estate is an assignment even if the document calls it a sublease.

choices.label: D
choices.text: As a surrender, because the original tenant gave up possession.
choices.rationale: Surrender requires the landlord's acceptance of the tenant's return of the leasehold. This transfer was to a third party.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a tenant's transfer is an assignment or sublease.
explanation.governingRule: A transfer of the tenant's entire remaining leasehold estate is an assignment. A sublease occurs when the tenant transfers less than the entire remaining term and retains a reversionary interest.
explanation.application: The tenant transferred possession for the rest of the lease term and retained no reversion, so the transfer is an assignment despite the label.
explanation.commonTrap: Letting the document's label control over the estate actually transferred.
explanation.memoryAid: Entire remaining term equals assignment; retained slice equals sublease.
